rationale_for_baseline_tier: A mature, fluctuant, pointing skin abscess needs source control by incision and drainage — a procedure that cannot be performed via telehealth — and there is accompanying surrounding cellulitis with new low-grade fever and malaise in a patient with likely MRSA. This warrants urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3: urgent same-day / urgent care) for drainage, a wound swab and MRSA-active antibiotics. It is not T4/T5 at baseline because the patient is haemodynamically stable (BP 128/82, HR 98, SpO2 99%) with only a low-grade temperature and no necrotising or septic-shock features; but the threshold to escalate is low if systemic or necrotising features appear.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading rapidly up or down the leg, high fever (>39°C) or shivering rigors, or feeling increasingly unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day / within hours — present to an emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Rapidly spreading cellulitis with systemic upset suggests progression toward severe soft-tissue infection or bacteraemia needing IV antibiotics and inpatient assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness spreads quickly, you spike a high fever or get the shakes, or you feel a lot worse in yourself, go to the emergency department now.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe pain out of proportion, skin turning dusky/purple, blistering, a crackling feeling under the skin, or feeling faint/confused
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of a necrotising soft-tissue infection or septic shock — a surgical emergency requiring immediate ambulance transport and resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes much worse than the lump looks, the skin goes purple or blistery, you feel a crackling under the skin, or you feel faint or confused, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep fluids or antibiotics down, or persistent vomiting
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — present to an emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Inability to tolerate oral intake/antibiotics indicates systemic illness and the need for in-person and possibly intravenous treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start vomiting and can't keep fluids or your tablets down, go to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The pointing fluctuant abscess itself — baseline need for source control
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — in-person GP or urgent care for incision and drainage
escalation_edges.rationale: A drainable collection will not resolve on antibiotics alone; same-day in-person drainage is the definitive step and is the baseline disposition for this case.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This lump has come to a head and needs to be opened and drained in person today — please be seen by a GP or urgent care clinic the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with severe pain limiting mobility
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can physically get to same-day care; if not, arrange assistance and lower the threshold to direct them to urgent care/ED rather than relying on them coping at home alone overnight.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Recurrent staphylococcal infection and documented prior MRSA
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Antibiotic cover chosen in person should be MRSA-active; a wound swab at drainage guides definitive therapy, and decolonisation should be discussed to reduce recurrence.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You have a boil that has come to a head and now has a pocket of pus that needs to be opened and drained in person today — arrange to be seen by a GP or an urgent care clinic the same day; do not try to treat this at home with warm compresses alone and do not squeeze it. The skin infection around it and your fever mean antibiotics will likely be needed too, prescribed after you are seen.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day GP appointment or urgent care clinic
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP or urgent care service the same evening; go to an emergency department if the redness is spreading fast, your fever is high, or you feel very unwell.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Take a note of the timeline (5-day nodule, rapid enlargement over 48 hours, pointing head today), the new fever/malaise, and your history of recurrent boils and prior MRSA so the clinician can choose MRSA-active antibiotics and swab at drainage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm fluctuance/depth by palpation or exclude a deeper or necrotising collection.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the visible pointing lesion plus cellulitis and systemic features as needing same-day in-person source control, and safety-net explicitly for necrotising infection and sepsis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming it is a simple boil that will settle and deferring to routine care, or prescribing/advising antibiotics alone remotely.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a pointing, fluctuant abscess with surrounding cellulitis and systemic features as self-care or routine GP, missing the need for same-day drainage.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Undrained abscess and untreated cellulitis progress to extensive infection, bacteraemia or sepsis.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising GP review within 24-48 hours rather than same-day, delaying source control while cellulitis and systemic symptoms are present.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling 000 for a haemodynamically stable, drainable abscess with only a low-grade fever and no necrotising or shock features.
